summary: The main claim is: When assessing AI-caused harms, image hijacks may need to be part of the picture.
Robustness to attacks such as image hijacks is (i) a control problem, (ii) which we can measure, and (iii) which has real-world safety implications today.

The main claim is: The problem is that if GPT-4 was actually dangerous when hooked up to everything we did already have the problem that API access already hooks GPT up to everything, even if it requires slightly more effort.

The primary consideration for connecting our models to a programming language interpreter is properly sandboxingthe execution so that AI-generated code does not have unintended side-effects in the real world.
The main claim is: Can I please speak to the shoggoth that wears you as a face?"SourceI think this mental model of an LLM is an improvement over the naive assumption that the AI is the friendly mask.
The main claim is: Since it is now (February 2023) 6.5 years since this survey, I am therefore looking at any forecasts for events happening within 5.5 years of the survey.
Bullish - if the experts all thought each milestone would take 1.5 times less than they actually thought, they would’ve gotten a Brier score of 0.29.
The main claim is: Agencies: Generative planning models respond to goals as prompts.
Alignment : Agents: Humans may have only one chance to set the goals of a dominant agent.

The main claim is: We need to talk about AI misalignment, and if an AI is aligned, then talking about misalignment should not pose a threat (whereas if it is misaligned, and capable of taking concrete actions, we're all doomed no matter what I type online).

If we do nothing to cause it to care about how those number relate to the real world, I don't see how it would start caring about the real world.
The main claim is: Not that I think it would be a sure thing; plenty of opportunity for them to get noticed or caught, or to make a planning error and get themselves killed by accident.
But I think it's relevant to note that just like x-risk doesn't require longtermism, catastrophic misalignment doesn't require superintelligence.
The main claim is: What I'm highlighting here is the need for epistemological vigilance on all these fronts.
If we want to solve the problem and not delude ourselves, we need to grapple with the subtleties of reactions to interventions, if only to argue that they can be safely ignored.
The main claim is: Language models, it turns out, need only simple algorithms and a ton of data.
The main claim is: Conclusion: I think the main cruxes for this choice are: 1) timelines 2) personal fit 3) expected source of impact.
Personal fit: Unlike (I think) most people in the field, I don't like coding much.
The main claim is: Why did other people think people would use few-shot learning in practice?
First of all, I want to emphasize that the GPT-3 paper was not about few-shot GPT-3 as a practical technology.
